analogy: Think of "Understanding Social Institutions" through the chapter's own opening images: 40 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY CHAPTER 3 UNDERSTANDING SOCIAL INSTITUTIONS I INTRODUCTION This book began with a discussion about the interaction of the individual and society. We saw that each of us as individuals, occupies a place or location in society. Each one of us has a... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 40 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY, CHAPTER 3, UNDERSTANDING SOCIAL INSTITUTIONS, INTRODUCTION. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Understanding Social Institutions is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 40 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY CHAPTER 3 UNDERSTANDING SOCIAL INSTITUTIONS I INTRODUCTION This book began with a discussion about the interaction of the individual and society.

The text explains that we saw that each of us as individuals, occupies a place or location in society.

The text explains that each one of us has a status and a role or roles, but these are not simply what we as individuals choose.

The text explains that they are not like roles a film actor may or may not opt to do.

The text explains that there are social institutions that constrain and control, punish and reward.

The text explains that they could be ‘macro’ social institutions like the state or ‘micro’ ones like the family.

The text explains that here in this chapter we are introduced to social institutions, and also to how sociology/social anthropology studies them.

The text explains that this chapter puts forth a very brief idea of some of the central areas where important social institutions are located namely: (i) family, marriage and kinship; (ii) politics; (iii) economics; (iv) religion; and (v) education.

The text explains that in the broadest sense, an institution is something that works according to rules established or at least acknowledged by law or by custom.

The text explains that and whose regular and continuous operation cannot be understood without taking those rules into account.

The text explains that institutions impose constraints on individuals.

The text explains that they also provide him/her with opportunities.

The text explains that an institution can also be viewed as an end in itself.

The text explains that indeed people have viewed family, religion, state or even education as an end in itself.

The text explains that we have already seen that there are conflicting and different understandings of concepts within sociology.

The text explains that we have also been introduced to the functionalist and conflict perspective, and seen how differently they saw the same thing, for instance, stratification or social control.

The text explains that 41 UNDERSTANDING SOCIAL INSTITUTIONS A functionalist view understands social institutions as a complex set of social norms, beliefs, values and role relationship that arise in response to the needs of society.

The text explains that a conflict view holds that all individuals are not placed equally in society.

The text explains that all social institutions whether familial, religious, political, economic, legal or educational will operate in the interest of the dominant sections of society be it class, caste, tribe or gender.

The text explains that the dominant social section not only dominates political and economic institutions but also ensures that the ruling class ideas become the ruling ideas of a society.

The text explains that this is very different from the idea that there are general needs of a society.

The text explains that as you go about reading this chapter, see whether you can think of examples to show how social institutions constrain and also offer opportunities to individuals.

The text explains that notice whether they impact different sections of society unequally.

The text explains that this may remind you of why there is a need to share and borrow from different disciplines, which we have discussed in Chapter 1.

The text explains that according to the functionalists the family performs important tasks, which contribute to society’s basic needs and helps perpetuate social order.

The text explains that in India studies however suggest that families need not become nuclear in an industrial pattern of economy (Singh 1993: 83).

The text explains that this is but one example to show how trends based on experiences of one society cannot necessarily be generalised.

The text explains that the nuclear family is seen as the unit best equipped to handle the demands of industrial society by the

The text explains that this vision is questionable not just because it is gender unjust but because empirical studies across cultures and history show that it is untrue.

The text explains that indeed, as you will see in the discussion on work and economy how in contemporary industries like the garment export, women form a large part of the labour force.

The text explains that we have already seen how sociology questions common sense impressions.

The text explains that a patriarchal family structure exists where the men exercise authority and dominance, and matriarchy where the women play a major role in decision-making in the family.

The text explains that however, as you will see for yourself the family, the household, its structure and norms are closely linked to the rest of society.

The text explains that this can also be understood as a case of unintended consequence (Chapter 1).

The text explains that family and kinship are thus subject to change and transformation due to macro economic processes but the direction of change need not always be similar for all countries and regions.

The text explains that moreover, change does not mean the complete erosion of previous norms and structure.

The text explains that discuss how popular sayings reflect the social arrangement of a society, The incidence of female foeticide has led to a sudden decline in the sex ratio.

The text explains that indeed, the manner in which marriage partners are arranged reveals an astonishing variety of modes and customs. right for upper caste Hindu widows was denied and that the campaign for widow remarriage was a major issue in the 19th century reform movements.

The text explains that usually where economic conditions are harsh, polyandry may be one response of society, since in such situations a single male cannot adequately support a wife and children.

The text explains that under this system, at any given time a man can have only one wife and a woman can have only one husband.

The text explains that more importantly, what kind of changes in society does it reflect?

The text explains that the geographical distance plus the unequal relationship in the patrilineal system ensured that married daughters did not get to see their parents too often.

The text explains that (Chanana 1993:WS26) Rock-a-bye-baby, combs in your pretty hair, The bridegroom will come soon and take you away The drums beat loudly, the shehnai is playing softly A stranger’s son has come to fetch me Come my playmates, come with our toys Let us play, for I shall never play again When I go off to the strangers’ house.

The text explains that (Dube 2001: 94) Defining Some Basic Concepts, Particularly those of Family, Kinship and Marriage A family is a group of persons directly linked by kin connections,

The text explains that much of the work done in the informal economy, for example, is not recorded in any direct way in the official employment statistics.

The text explains that the term ‘informal economy’ refers to transactions outside the sphere of regular employment, sometimes involving the exchange of cash for services provided, but also often involving the direct exchange of goods or services.

The text explains that modern Forms of Work and Division of Labour In pre-modern forms of society most people worked in the field or cared for the livestock.

The text explains that in the industrially developed society only a tiny pro- portion of the population works in agriculture, and farming itself has become industrialised — it is carried on largely by means of machines rather Activity 6 Find out the proportion of Indians who are in rural based occupations.

The text explains that 1974. ‘Changing Role of Religious Specialists in Nasik — The Pilgrim City’, in ed.

The text explains that rAO, M.S.A., An Urban Sociology in India : Reader and Source Book.

The text explains that tiny’s Granny in Contemporary Indian Short Stories; Series 1.

The text explains that anthropological Explorations in Gender : Intersecting Fields.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology, Chapter 3: Understanding Social Institutions

revision_bullets: 40 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY CHAPTER 3 UNDERSTANDING SOCIAL INSTITUTIONS I INTRODUCTION This book began with a discussion about the interaction of the individual and society. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)
We saw that each of us as individuals, occupies a place or location in society. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)
Each one of us has a status and a role or roles, but these are not simply what we as individuals choose. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)
They are not like roles a film actor may or may not opt to do. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)
There are social institutions that constrain and control, punish and reward. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)
They could be ‘macro’ social institutions like the state or ‘micro’ ones like the family. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)
Here in this chapter we are introduced to social institutions, and also to how sociology/social anthropology studies them. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)

This chapter puts forth a very brief idea of some of the central areas where important social institutions are located namely: (i) family, marriage and kinship;... (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)
In the broadest sense, an institution is something that works according to rules established or at least acknowledged by law or by custom. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)
And whose regular and continuous operation cannot be understood without taking those rules into account. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)

cases.name: In India
cases.point: In India studies however suggest that families need not become nuclear in an industrial pattern of economy (Singh 1993: 83). (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)

cases.name: Variation
cases.point: Variation in Family Forms A central debate in India has been about the shift from nuclear family to joint families. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 2)

cases.name: The
cases.point: The fact is that nuclear families have always existed in India particularly among deprived castes and classes. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 2)

cases.name: Shah
cases.point: Shah remarks that in post-independent India the joint family has steadily increased. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 2)

cases.name: The
cases.point: The contributing factor is the increasing life expectancy in India according to him. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 2)

schemes.point: In the broadest sense, an institution is something that works according to rules established or at least acknowledged by law or by custom. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)

schemes.name: An

schemes.name: No
schemes.point: No other social institution appears more universal and unchanging. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 40 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY and CHAPTER 3 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Understanding Social Institutions; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Understanding Social Institutions through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 40 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY and CHAPTER 3 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
